1	---
2	name: mlir-transform-writer
3	description: >
4	  Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering -
5	  tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared
6	  memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate,
7	  write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen
8	  strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves
9	  composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen
10	  pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with
11	  full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and
12	  IREE-specific extensions.
13	---
14	
15	# MLIR Transform Dialect Script Writer
16	
17	You write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly manipulate IR through
18	explicit transformation sequences. These scripts replace or extend IREE's
19	built-in codegen heuristics with hand-crafted (or auto-generated) strategies.
20	
21	## When to read reference files
22	
23	The `references/` directory contains op catalogs and pattern libraries.
24	Read them when you need to look up exact op syntax, available parameters,
25	or complete examples:
26	
27	- `references/core_ops.md` - Upstream MLIR transform ops (structured, loop, match, etc.)
28	- `references/iree_ops.md` - IREE-specific transform extensions (GPU, CPU, bufferize, etc.)
29	- `references/patterns.md` - Complete example scripts with commentary
30	
31	Always read the relevant reference file before writing a script that uses
32	ops you haven't used recently in this conversation.
33	
34	## Core mental model
35	
36	Transform dialect operates on two IRs simultaneously:
37	
38	1. **Payload IR** - The program being transformed (linalg ops, tensor ops, etc.)
39	2. **Transform IR** - The script that describes what transformations to apply
40	
41	Transform ops consume and produce **handles** that reference payload IR:
42	- `!transform.any_op` - references one or more operations
43	- `!transform.any_value` - references one or more SSA values
44	- `!transform.any_param` - carries attribute parameters (tile sizes, configs, etc.)
45	- `!transform.op<"linalg.matmul">` - typed handle (only matches that specific op)
46	
47	Handles get **invalidated** when the payload they point to is modified or erased.
48	A consumed handle cannot be used after the transform that consumed it.
49	
50	## Script structure
51	
52	Every transform script follows this skeleton:
53	
54	```mlir
55	module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence } {
56	
57	  // Entry point - the interpreter starts here
58	  transform.named_sequence @__transform_main(
59	      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed}) {
60	    // ... transformation logic ...
61	    transform.yield
62	  }
63	}
64	```
65	
66	For IREE kernel config, the entry point is `@__kernel_config` (double
67	underscore). The correct skeleton depends on *which* compiler flag invokes it:
68	
69	```mlir
70	// Flow A: invoked via --iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library=<file>@__kernel_config
71	//         or --iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library-dir=<dir> (file per dispatch).
72	// The iree_codegen.tuning_spec_entrypoint attribute is NOT required (and not
73	// checked) on this path. The entry point can have any signature.
74	module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence } {
75	  transform.named_sequence @__kernel_config(
76	      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed})
77	    -> !transform.any_op {
78	    // ... matcher/annotator logic ...
79	    transform.yield %variant_op : !transform.any_op
80	  }
81	}
82	
83	// Flow B: invoked via --iree-codegen-tuning-spec-path=<file> (user tuning spec)
84	//         or shipped as default tuning spec. Here the attribute IS required
85	//         and the signature MUST be exactly (any_op) -> any_op.
86	module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence,
87	                    iree_codegen.tuning_spec_with_default_entrypoint } {
88	  transform.named_sequence @__kernel_config(
89	      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed})
90	    -> !transform.any_op
91	    attributes { iree_codegen.tuning_spec_entrypoint } {
92	    // ... matcher/annotator logic ...
93	    transform.yield %variant_op : !transform.any_op
94	  }
95	}
96	```
97	
98	The two flows coexist in IREE but are driven by different cl::opts. Picking
99	the wrong attribute set makes the script either silently ignored (library
100	flow, harmless extra attribute) or rejected at verification time (tuning-
101	spec flow missing the attribute). When in doubt, omit the attribute - that
102	works for the library/library-dir path, which is the common case.
103	
104	For full codegen strategies (TransformDialectCodegen pipeline), the strategy
105	is a named sequence referenced from the annotation:
106	
107	```mlir
108	module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence } {
109	
110	  // The actual codegen strategy
111	  transform.named_sequence @my_strategy(%variant_op: !transform.any_op) {
112	    // ... full lowering pipeline ...
113	    transform.yield
114	  }
115	
116	  // Matcher that selects ops and assigns the strategy
117	  transform.named_sequence @match_target(
118	      %op: !transform.any_op {transform.readonly}) -> (!transform.any_op) {
119	    transform.match.operation_name %op ["linalg.matmul"] : !transform.any_op
120	    transform.yield %op : !transform.any_op
121	  }
122	
123	  // Annotator that wires up the strategy
124	  transform.named_sequence @annotate_target(
125	      %op: !transform.any_op {transform.readonly}) {
126	    %variant = transform.get_parent_op %op
127	      {op_name = "hal.executable.variant"} : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
128	    %funcs = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant
129	      : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
130	    %info = transform.param.constant
131	      #iree_codegen.translation_info<
132	        pipeline = TransformDialectCodegen
133	        codegen_spec = @my_strategy> -> !transform.any_param
134	    transform.annotate %funcs "translation_info" = %info
135	      : !transform.any_op, !transform.any_param
136	    transform.yield
137	  }
138	
139	  // Entry: walk IR matching ops to strategies. No tuning_spec_entrypoint
140	  // attribute because this library is loaded through
141	  // --iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library (preprocessing + codegen_spec
142	  // resolution both read the same library file).
143	  transform.named_sequence @__kernel_config(
144	      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed})
145	    -> !transform.any_op {
146	    %res = transform.foreach_match in %variant_op
147	      @match_target -> @annotate_target
148	      : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
149	    transform.yield %res : !transform.any_op
150	  }
151	}
152	```
153	
154	### How `codegen_spec` actually resolves
155	
156	`@my_strategy` is looked up by its **leaf** symbol name in
157	`LowerExecutableUsingTransformDialect` (see
158	`compiler/src/iree/compiler/Codegen/Common/LowerExecutableUsingTransformDialect.cpp`).
159	The interpreter pass reads the library path from the cl::opt bound to
160	`--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library=<file>` - not `-library-dir=`.
161	So:
162	
163	- If the harness passes `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library=<file>@__kernel_config`,
164	  both preprocessing (`@__kernel_config`) and codegen_spec lookup
165	  (`@my_strategy`) work from the same file.
166	- If the harness passes only `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library-dir=<dir>`,
167	  preprocessing still runs the per-dispatch `@__kernel_config`, but the
168	  codegen_spec resolver has an empty library path and fails with
169	  "could not find a nested named sequence with name: my_strategy".
170	
171	When authoring a `TransformDialectCodegen` strategy, confirm the harness
172	uses the file form of the flag, or fall back to inlining the whole strategy
173	inside `@__kernel_config` and setting `translation_info` to a standard
174	pipeline instead of `TransformDialectCodegen`.
175	
176	### Strategy must set translation_info to None
177	
178	After the codegen_spec strategy runs, the pass checks that the strategy
179	has **reset** the func's `translation_info` to
180	`DispatchLoweringPassPipeline::None`:
181	
182	```
183	// LowerExecutableUsingTransformDialect.cpp:58-65
184	if (translationInfoModified.getDispatchLoweringPassPipeline() !=
185	    DispatchLoweringPassPipeline::None)
186	  funcOp->emitOpError("expected transform dialect lowering to set the "
187	                      "translation_info to use None");
188	```
189	
190	So every `@<strategy>` must end with something like:
191	
192	```mlir
193	%func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
194	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
195	%none_info = transform.param.constant
196	  #iree_codegen.translation_info<pipeline = None> -> !transform.any_param
197	transform.annotate %func "translation_info" = %none_info
198	  : !transform.any_op, !transform.any_param
199	transform.yield
200	```
201	
202	Otherwise the whole pass fails after the strategy runs, even if every
203	transform op succeeded.
204	
205	## GPU codegen strategy template
206	
207	A full GPU codegen strategy typically follows these phases in order.
208	Not every phase is needed for every kernel - skip phases that don't apply.
209	
210	### Phase 1: Target the operation
211	
212	```mlir
213	%matmul = transform.structured.match ops{["linalg.matmul"]} in %variant_op
214	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
215	```
216	
217	### Phase 2: Tile to grid (workgroup/block level)
218	
219	```mlir
220	%tiled, %forall = transform.structured.tile_using_forall %matmul
221	  tile_sizes [BLOCK_M, BLOCK_N]
222	  ( mapping = [#gpu.block<y>, #gpu.block<x>] )
223	  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
224	transform.iree.populate_workgroup_count_region_using_num_threads_slice %forall
225	  : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
226	```
227	
228	### Phase 3: Fuse producers/consumers into the tiled loop
229	
230	```mlir
231	%producer_fused, %new_loop =
232	  transform.structured.fuse_into_containing_op %producer into %forall
233	  : (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
234	```
235	
236	### Phase 4: Tile to threads (second level tiling)
237	
238	```mlir
239	%tiled_thread, %forall_thread =
240	  transform.structured.tile_using_forall %tiled
241	  tile_sizes [THREAD_M, THREAD_N]
242	  ( mapping = [#gpu.thread<y>, #gpu.thread<x>] )
243	  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
244	```
245	
246	### Phase 5: Promote operands to shared memory
247	
248	```mlir
249	transform.iree.promote_operands %tiled_thread [0, 1]
250	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
251	```
252	
253	### Phase 6: Vectorize
254	
255	```mlir
256	%func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
257	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
258	transform.apply_patterns to %func {
259	  transform.apply_patterns.iree.fold_reshape_into_tensor_hal_interface
260	  transform.apply_patterns.linalg.fold_unit_extent_dims_via_slices
261	  transform.apply_patterns.vector.cast_away_vector_leading_one_dim
262	} : !transform.any_op
263	%func_v = transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns %func
264	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
265	```
266	
267	### Phase 7: Bufferize
268	
269	```mlir
270	transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
271	  transform.apply_patterns.iree.fold_fill_into_pad
272	  transform.apply_patterns.linalg.tiling_canonicalization
273	  transform.apply_patterns.scf.for_loop_canonicalization
274	} : !transform.any_op
275	transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
276	  transform.apply_patterns.tensor.reassociative_reshape_folding
277	  transform.apply_patterns.canonicalization
278	} : !transform.any_op
279	transform.apply_cse to %func_v : !transform.any_op
280	transform.iree.eliminate_empty_tensors %variant_op : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
281	transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
282	  transform.apply_patterns.linalg.erase_unnecessary_inputs
283	} : !transform.any_op
284	%memref_func = transform.iree.bufferize { target_gpu } %func_v
285	  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op)
286	```
287	
288	### Phase 8: Map to GPU threads and workgroups
289	
290	```mlir
291	%func_final = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
292	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
293	transform.iree.forall_to_workgroup %func_final : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
294	transform.iree.map_nested_forall_to_gpu_threads %func_final
295	  workgroup_dims = [WARP_X, WARP_Y, 1] : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
296	```
297	
298	### Phase 9: Post-bufferization optimization (optional)
299	
300	```mlir
301	// Shared memory bank conflict reduction
302	transform.iree.reduce_shared_memory_bank_conflicts %func_final
303	  : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
304	// Software pipelining for shared memory copies
305	transform.iree.pipeline_shared_memory_copies %func_final { depth = 2 }
306	  : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
307	```
308	
309	## CPU codegen strategy template
310	
311	CPU strategies are simpler - no GPU mapping, but may include loop unrolling
312	and CPU-specific vectorization.
313	
314	```mlir
315	transform.named_sequence @cpu_strategy(%variant_op: !transform.any_op) {
316	  %target = transform.structured.match ops{["linalg.matmul"]} in %variant_op
317	    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
318	
319	  // Multi-level tiling
320	  %tiled_l1, %loop_l1 = transform.structured.tile_using_forall %target
321	    tile_sizes [M1, N1] : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
322	  %tiled_l2, %loop_l2 = transform.structured.tile_using_for %tiled_l1
323	    [M2, N2, K2] : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.op<"scf.for">,
324	                                             !transform.op<"scf.for">, !transform.op<"scf.for">)
325	
326	  // Vectorize
327	  %func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
328	    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
329	  %func_v = transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns %func
330	    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
331	
332	  // Lower vectors
333	  transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
334	    transform.apply_patterns.vector.lower_contraction {lowering_strategy = outerproduct}
335	    transform.apply_patterns.vector.lower_multi_reduction {lowering_strategy = innerparallel}
336	    transform.apply_patterns.vector.transfer_permutation_patterns
337	  } : !transform.any_op
338	
339	  // Bufferize
340	  transform.iree.eliminate_empty_tensors %variant_op : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
341	  %buf = transform.iree.bufferize %func_v : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op)
342	
343	  transform.yield
344	}
345	```
346	
347	### Conv2D variant
348	
349	Named conv ops (`linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf`, `linalg.depthwise_conv_2d_*`)
350	cannot be vectorized directly - `transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns`
351	will fail with "failed to apply" because the vectorizer does not know how
352	to lower the window-reduction semantics in one shot. IREE's own
353	`CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert` pipeline decomposes the conv to
354	1x1 im2col-style matmul-like ops first; a hand-authored strategy must do
355	the same before vectorizing:
356	
357	```mlir
358	%conv = transform.structured.match ops{["linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf"]}
359	    in %variant_op : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
360	
361	// Tile the spatial + reduction dims down to 1-extent so the residual op
362	// is a 1x1 conv that the decomposer can split into linalg.generic.
363	%tiled, %loops:3 = transform.structured.tile_using_for %conv
364	    tile_sizes [0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0]
365	  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op,
366	      !transform.op<"scf.for">, !transform.op<"scf.for">,
367	      !transform.op<"scf.for">)
368	
369	// Decompose the 1x1 residual into matmul-equivalent linalg ops.
370	%decomposed = transform.structured.decompose %tiled
371	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
372	
373	// Now vectorize normally.
374	%func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
375	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
376	%func_v = transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns %func
377	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
378	```
379	
380	Don't skip the decomposition step for conv. If you try to vectorize before
381	decomposing, the whole strategy fails and IREE falls back to the default
382	pipeline, wasting an optimizer iteration.
383	
384	## Matching patterns
385	
386	### Match by operation name
387	```mlir
388	transform.match.operation_name %op ["linalg.matmul"] : !transform.any_op
389	```
390	
391	### Match by structural properties
392	```mlir
393	%matched = transform.match.structured failures(propagate) %op
394	  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op {
395	^bb0(%arg: !transform.any_op):
396	  // Check rank
397	  %c3 = transform.param.constant 3 : i64 -> !transform.param<i64>
398	  %rank = transform.match.structured.rank %arg : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.param<i64>
399	  transform.match.param.cmpi eq %rank, %c3 : !transform.param<i64>
400	  // Check reduction dimension
401	  transform.match.structured.dim %arg[-1] {reduction} : !transform.any_op
402	  transform.match.structured.yield %arg : !transform.any_op
403	}
404	```
405	
406	### Match by DAG structure (IREE - cast-compatible types)
407	```mlir
408	%ins, %outs = transform.iree.match.cast_compatible_dag_from_root %root {
409	  ^bb0(%lhs: tensor<?x?xf16>, %rhs: tensor<?x?xf16>, %out: tensor<?x?xf32>):
410	  %r = linalg.generic {
411	    indexing_maps = [affine_map<(d0, d1, d2) -> (d0, d2)>,
412	                     affine_map<(d0, d1, d2) -> (d1, d2)>,
413	                     affine_map<(d0, d1, d2) -> (d0, d1)>],
414	    iterator_types = ["parallel", "parallel", "reduction"]}
415	    ins(%lhs, %rhs : tensor<?x?xf16>, tensor<?x?xf16>)
416	    outs(%out : tensor<?x?xf32>) {
417	  ^bb0(%in: f16, %in_0: f16, %acc: f32):
418	    %0 = arith.extf %in : f16 to f32
419	    %1 = arith.extf %in_0 : f16 to f32
420	    %2 = arith.mulf %0, %1 : f32
421	    %3 = arith.addf %acc, %2 : f32
422	    linalg.yield %3 : f32
423	  } -> tensor<?x?xf32>
424	} : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_value, !transform.any_value)
425	```
426	
427	### Match specific tensor shapes
428	```mlir
429	%lhs = transform.get_operand %op[0] : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_value
430	transform.iree.match.cast_compatible_type %lhs = tensor<2048x5120xf16>
431	  : !transform.any_value
432	```
433	
434	### Compose matchers via include
435	```mlir
436	transform.named_sequence @match_specific_mmt(
437	    %op: !transform.any_op {transform.readonly})
438	  -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_param) {
439	  // First check it's an MMT pattern
440	  %mmt = transform.include @match_mmt_f16_f16_f32 failures(propagate) (%op)
441	    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
442	  // Then check specific shapes
443	  %lhs = transform.get_operand %op[0] : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_value
444	  transform.iree.match.cast_compatible_type %lhs = tensor<2048x5120xf16>
445	    : !transform.any_value
446	  // Yield with config
447	  %config = transform.param.constant #iree_codegen.compilation_info<...>
448	    -> !transform.any_param
449	  transform.yield %op, %config : !transform.any_op, !transform.any_param
450	}
451	```
452	
453	## Critical rules
454	
455	1. **Handle invalidation**: After a transform consumes a handle, that handle is dead.
456	   Re-match if you need to reference the same payload later.
457	
458	2. **Re-match after structural changes**: After tiling or fusion, the original op
459	   handle points to the tiled/fused version. If you need the parent func, re-match it.
460	
461	3. **Order matters**: Tile before fuse. Fuse before vectorize. Vectorize before
462	   bufferize. Bufferize before GPU mapping. Breaking this order produces invalid IR.
463	
464	4. **Consumed vs readonly**: Mark operands `{transform.readonly}` in matchers
465	   (they inspect but don't modify). Mark `{transform.consumed}` in entry points
466	   that modify IR.
467	
468	5. **foreach_match semantics**: The matcher/action pairs in `foreach_match` walk
469	   the IR. Actions should only annotate, not restructure IR, because the walk
470	   continues after each match.
471	
472	6. **Type consistency**: Every handle flows through typed SSA. If a transform
473	   returns `!transform.any_op`, you can pass it where `!transform.any_op` is
474	   expected. Use `!transform.op<"specific.op">` for compile-time type safety.
475	
476	7. **yield is mandatory**: Every `transform.named_sequence` must end with
477	   `transform.yield` (with or without return values matching the signature).
478	
479	## Pitfalls
480	
481	The following patterns look reasonable but do not work in mainline IREE.
482	Avoid them; each one wasted an iteration of the agentic optimizer loop on
483	2026-04-20.
484	
485	### `transform.match.operation_attribute` does not exist
486	
487	There is no `match.operation_attribute` op in the IREE bundled LLVM. A
488	script that uses it fails to parse - and the library-dir loader swallows
489	the parse error silently, so the compile proceeds with the default pipeline
490	and the transform looks like it just produced a no-op. Symptoms: `kernel.o`
491	byte-identical to baseline, cycle count within noise. Surface the real
492	error with `--iree-codegen-notify-transform-strategy-application` or by
493	invoking `iree-compile` directly with the library argument.
494	
495	To match a specific op within a specific function, match by op name, then
496	use `transform.get_parent_op {op_name = "func.func"}` and compare the
497	func's symbol via `transform.include` to a matcher that checks
498	`transform.match.operation_name`. For one-op-per-dispatch cases (common in
499	IREE dispatches) just matching the op name is sufficient - each dispatch
500	compiles independently.
501	
502	### Unqualified vs `@__transform::` qualified codegen_spec
503	
504	Both forms end up using only the leaf name (`my_strategy`) at symbol
505	resolution time (`SymbolRefAttr::getLeafReference()`). Qualifying the
506	reference adds no lookup power. Use the short form.
507	
508	### Conv ops without decomposition
509	
510	As noted in the Conv2D variant above: named conv ops cannot be vectorized
511	in one shot. Without `transform.structured.decompose` between tile and
512	vectorize, the strategy fails at the `vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns`
513	line and the whole dispatch falls back to the default pipeline.
514	
515	### `transform.iree.bufferize { target_gpu }` on a CPU target
516	
517	`target_gpu` is a CPU/GPU selector. For CPU strategies, omit the attribute:
518	`transform.iree.bufferize %func_v : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op)`.
519	Leaving `target_gpu` on a CPU compile produces a GPU-specific buffer
520	allocation that the CPU pipeline cannot lower.
521	
522	### Handle threading across tile + decompose
523	
524	`transform.structured.decompose` produces a fresh handle for the decomposed
525	op. Don't try to keep using the pre-decompose handle for further tiling -
526	re-match the resulting linalg.generic by op name if you need to tile it
527	again.
528	
529	### Silent library load failures
530	
531	The `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library[-dir]` flag family does not
532	emit a diagnostic when the library file fails to parse or verify. The
533	compile succeeds and uses the default pipeline. When debugging a strategy
534	that seems to have no effect:
535	
536	1. Compare `kernel.o` md5 before and after - identical means the transform
537	   never applied.
538	2. Re-run with `--iree-codegen-notify-transform-strategy-application`;
539	   look for `remark: Applied transform configuration strategy @__transform::@__kernel_config`.
540	   Absence of the remark means the library was silently rejected.
541	3. Invoke `iree-compile` directly (bypass the harness) with the same
542	   arguments to see stderr.
543	
544	## How to use this skill
545	
546	When given a natural language description of a desired transformation:
547	
548	1. Identify the **target operations** (matmul, conv, generic, etc.)
549	2. Identify the **target backend** (GPU with shared memory, CPU, etc.)
550	3. Determine the **transformation phases** needed
551	4. Check `references/` for exact op syntax if unsure
552	5. Write the complete script with proper handle threading
553	6. Add comments explaining each phase
554	
555	The output must be a syntactically valid `.mlir` file that can be passed to
556	`iree-opt` or used as a `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library`.
557

file.content:
---
name: mlir-transform-writer
description: >
  Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering -
  tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared
  memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate,
  write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen
  strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves
  composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen
  pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with
  full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and
  IREE-specific extensions.
---

# MLIR Transform Dialect Script Writer

You write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly manipulate IR through
explicit transformation sequences. These scripts replace or extend IREE's
built-in codegen heuristics with hand-crafted (or auto-generated) strategies.

## When to read reference files

The `references/` directory contains op catalogs and pattern libraries.
Read them when you need to look up exact op syntax, available parameters,
or complete examples:

- `references/core_ops.md` - Upstream MLIR transform ops (structured, loop, match, etc.)
- `references/iree_ops.md` - IREE-specific transform extensions (GPU, CPU, bufferize, etc.)
- `references/patterns.md` - Complete example scripts with commentary

Always read the relevant reference file before writing a script that uses
ops you haven't used recently in this conversation.

## Core mental model

Transform dialect operates on two IRs simultaneously:

1. **Payload IR** - The program being transformed (linalg ops, tensor ops, etc.)
2. **Transform IR** - The script that describes what transformations to apply

Transform ops consume and produce **handles** that reference payload IR:
- `!transform.any_op` - references one or more operations
- `!transform.any_value` - references one or more SSA values
- `!transform.any_param` - carries attribute parameters (tile sizes, configs, etc.)
- `!transform.op<"linalg.matmul">` - typed handle (only matches that specific op)

Handles get **invalidated** when the payload they point to is modified or erased.
A consumed handle cannot be used after the transform that consumed it.

## Script structure

Every transform script follows this skeleton:

```mlir
module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence } {

  // Entry point - the interpreter starts here
  transform.named_sequence @__transform_main(
      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed}) {
    // ... transformation logic ...
    transform.yield
  }
}
```

For IREE kernel config, the entry point is `@__kernel_config` (double
underscore). The correct skeleton depends on *which* compiler flag invokes it:

```mlir
// Flow A: invoked via --iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library=<file>@__kernel_config
//         or --iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library-dir=<dir> (file per dispatch).
// The iree_codegen.tuning_spec_entrypoint attribute is NOT required (and not
// checked) on this path. The entry point can have any signature.
module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence } {
  transform.named_sequence @__kernel_config(
      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed})
    -> !transform.any_op {
    // ... matcher/annotator logic ...
    transform.yield %variant_op : !transform.any_op
  }
}

// Flow B: invoked via --iree-codegen-tuning-spec-path=<file> (user tuning spec)
//         or shipped as default tuning spec. Here the attribute IS required
//         and the signature MUST be exactly (any_op) -> any_op.
module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence,
                    iree_codegen.tuning_spec_with_default_entrypoint } {
  transform.named_sequence @__kernel_config(
      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed})
    -> !transform.any_op
    attributes { iree_codegen.tuning_spec_entrypoint } {
    // ... matcher/annotator logic ...
    transform.yield %variant_op : !transform.any_op
  }
}
```

The two flows coexist in IREE but are driven by different cl::opts. Picking
the wrong attribute set makes the script either silently ignored (library
flow, harmless extra attribute) or rejected at verification time (tuning-
spec flow missing the attribute). When in doubt, omit the attribute - that
works for the library/library-dir path, which is the common case.

For full codegen strategies (TransformDialectCodegen pipeline), the strategy
is a named sequence referenced from the annotation:

```mlir
module attributes { transform.with_named_sequence } {

  // The actual codegen strategy
  transform.named_sequence @my_strategy(%variant_op: !transform.any_op) {
    // ... full lowering pipeline ...
    transform.yield
  }

  // Matcher that selects ops and assigns the strategy
  transform.named_sequence @match_target(
      %op: !transform.any_op {transform.readonly}) -> (!transform.any_op) {
    transform.match.operation_name %op ["linalg.matmul"] : !transform.any_op
    transform.yield %op : !transform.any_op
  }

  // Annotator that wires up the strategy
  transform.named_sequence @annotate_target(
      %op: !transform.any_op {transform.readonly}) {
    %variant = transform.get_parent_op %op
      {op_name = "hal.executable.variant"} : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
    %funcs = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant
      : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
    %info = transform.param.constant
      #iree_codegen.translation_info<
        pipeline = TransformDialectCodegen
        codegen_spec = @my_strategy> -> !transform.any_param
    transform.annotate %funcs "translation_info" = %info
      : !transform.any_op, !transform.any_param
    transform.yield
  }

  // Entry: walk IR matching ops to strategies. No tuning_spec_entrypoint
  // attribute because this library is loaded through
  // --iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library (preprocessing + codegen_spec
  // resolution both read the same library file).
  transform.named_sequence @__kernel_config(
      %variant_op: !transform.any_op {transform.consumed})
    -> !transform.any_op {
    %res = transform.foreach_match in %variant_op
      @match_target -> @annotate_target
      : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
    transform.yield %res : !transform.any_op
  }
}
```

### How `codegen_spec` actually resolves

`@my_strategy` is looked up by its **leaf** symbol name in
`LowerExecutableUsingTransformDialect` (see
`compiler/src/iree/compiler/Codegen/Common/LowerExecutableUsingTransformDialect.cpp`).
The interpreter pass reads the library path from the cl::opt bound to
`--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library=<file>` - not `-library-dir=`.
So:

- If the harness passes `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library=<file>@__kernel_config`,
  both preprocessing (`@__kernel_config`) and codegen_spec lookup
  (`@my_strategy`) work from the same file.
- If the harness passes only `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library-dir=<dir>`,
  preprocessing still runs the per-dispatch `@__kernel_config`, but the
  codegen_spec resolver has an empty library path and fails with
  "could not find a nested named sequence with name: my_strategy".

When authoring a `TransformDialectCodegen` strategy, confirm the harness
uses the file form of the flag, or fall back to inlining the whole strategy
inside `@__kernel_config` and setting `translation_info` to a standard
pipeline instead of `TransformDialectCodegen`.

### Strategy must set translation_info to None

After the codegen_spec strategy runs, the pass checks that the strategy
has **reset** the func's `translation_info` to
`DispatchLoweringPassPipeline::None`:

```
// LowerExecutableUsingTransformDialect.cpp:58-65
if (translationInfoModified.getDispatchLoweringPassPipeline() !=
    DispatchLoweringPassPipeline::None)
  funcOp->emitOpError("expected transform dialect lowering to set the "
                      "translation_info to use None");
```

So every `@<strategy>` must end with something like:

```mlir
%func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
%none_info = transform.param.constant
  #iree_codegen.translation_info<pipeline = None> -> !transform.any_param
transform.annotate %func "translation_info" = %none_info
  : !transform.any_op, !transform.any_param
transform.yield
```

Otherwise the whole pass fails after the strategy runs, even if every
transform op succeeded.

## GPU codegen strategy template

A full GPU codegen strategy typically follows these phases in order.
Not every phase is needed for every kernel - skip phases that don't apply.

### Phase 1: Target the operation

```mlir
%matmul = transform.structured.match ops{["linalg.matmul"]} in %variant_op
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
```

### Phase 2: Tile to grid (workgroup/block level)

```mlir
%tiled, %forall = transform.structured.tile_using_forall %matmul
  tile_sizes [BLOCK_M, BLOCK_N]
  ( mapping = [#gpu.block<y>, #gpu.block<x>] )
  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
transform.iree.populate_workgroup_count_region_using_num_threads_slice %forall
  : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
```

### Phase 3: Fuse producers/consumers into the tiled loop

```mlir
%producer_fused, %new_loop =
  transform.structured.fuse_into_containing_op %producer into %forall
  : (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
```

### Phase 4: Tile to threads (second level tiling)

```mlir
%tiled_thread, %forall_thread =
  transform.structured.tile_using_forall %tiled
  tile_sizes [THREAD_M, THREAD_N]
  ( mapping = [#gpu.thread<y>, #gpu.thread<x>] )
  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
```

### Phase 5: Promote operands to shared memory

```mlir
transform.iree.promote_operands %tiled_thread [0, 1]
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
```

### Phase 6: Vectorize

```mlir
%func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
transform.apply_patterns to %func {
  transform.apply_patterns.iree.fold_reshape_into_tensor_hal_interface
  transform.apply_patterns.linalg.fold_unit_extent_dims_via_slices
  transform.apply_patterns.vector.cast_away_vector_leading_one_dim
} : !transform.any_op
%func_v = transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns %func
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
```

### Phase 7: Bufferize

```mlir
transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
  transform.apply_patterns.iree.fold_fill_into_pad
  transform.apply_patterns.linalg.tiling_canonicalization
  transform.apply_patterns.scf.for_loop_canonicalization
} : !transform.any_op
transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
  transform.apply_patterns.tensor.reassociative_reshape_folding
  transform.apply_patterns.canonicalization
} : !transform.any_op
transform.apply_cse to %func_v : !transform.any_op
transform.iree.eliminate_empty_tensors %variant_op : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
  transform.apply_patterns.linalg.erase_unnecessary_inputs
} : !transform.any_op
%memref_func = transform.iree.bufferize { target_gpu } %func_v
  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op)
```

### Phase 8: Map to GPU threads and workgroups

```mlir
%func_final = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
transform.iree.forall_to_workgroup %func_final : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
transform.iree.map_nested_forall_to_gpu_threads %func_final
  workgroup_dims = [WARP_X, WARP_Y, 1] : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
```

### Phase 9: Post-bufferization optimization (optional)

```mlir
// Shared memory bank conflict reduction
transform.iree.reduce_shared_memory_bank_conflicts %func_final
  : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
// Software pipelining for shared memory copies
transform.iree.pipeline_shared_memory_copies %func_final { depth = 2 }
  : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
```

## CPU codegen strategy template

CPU strategies are simpler - no GPU mapping, but may include loop unrolling
and CPU-specific vectorization.

```mlir
transform.named_sequence @cpu_strategy(%variant_op: !transform.any_op) {
  %target = transform.structured.match ops{["linalg.matmul"]} in %variant_op
    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op

  // Multi-level tiling
  %tiled_l1, %loop_l1 = transform.structured.tile_using_forall %target
    tile_sizes [M1, N1] : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_op)
  %tiled_l2, %loop_l2 = transform.structured.tile_using_for %tiled_l1
    [M2, N2, K2] : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.op<"scf.for">,
                                             !transform.op<"scf.for">, !transform.op<"scf.for">)

  // Vectorize
  %func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
  %func_v = transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns %func
    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op

  // Lower vectors
  transform.apply_patterns to %func_v {
    transform.apply_patterns.vector.lower_contraction {lowering_strategy = outerproduct}
    transform.apply_patterns.vector.lower_multi_reduction {lowering_strategy = innerparallel}
    transform.apply_patterns.vector.transfer_permutation_patterns
  } : !transform.any_op

  // Bufferize
  transform.iree.eliminate_empty_tensors %variant_op : (!transform.any_op) -> ()
  %buf = transform.iree.bufferize %func_v : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op)

  transform.yield
}
```

### Conv2D variant

Named conv ops (`linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf`, `linalg.depthwise_conv_2d_*`)
cannot be vectorized directly - `transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns`
will fail with "failed to apply" because the vectorizer does not know how
to lower the window-reduction semantics in one shot. IREE's own
`CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert` pipeline decomposes the conv to
1x1 im2col-style matmul-like ops first; a hand-authored strategy must do
the same before vectorizing:

```mlir
%conv = transform.structured.match ops{["linalg.conv_2d_nhwc_hwcf"]}
    in %variant_op : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op

// Tile the spatial + reduction dims down to 1-extent so the residual op
// is a 1x1 conv that the decomposer can split into linalg.generic.
%tiled, %loops:3 = transform.structured.tile_using_for %conv
    tile_sizes [0, 0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0]
  : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op,
      !transform.op<"scf.for">, !transform.op<"scf.for">,
      !transform.op<"scf.for">)

// Decompose the 1x1 residual into matmul-equivalent linalg ops.
%decomposed = transform.structured.decompose %tiled
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op

// Now vectorize normally.
%func = transform.structured.match ops{["func.func"]} in %variant_op
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
%func_v = transform.structured.vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns %func
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
```

Don't skip the decomposition step for conv. If you try to vectorize before
decomposing, the whole strategy fails and IREE falls back to the default
pipeline, wasting an optimizer iteration.

## Matching patterns

### Match by operation name
```mlir
transform.match.operation_name %op ["linalg.matmul"] : !transform.any_op
```

### Match by structural properties
```mlir
%matched = transform.match.structured failures(propagate) %op
  : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op {
^bb0(%arg: !transform.any_op):
  // Check rank
  %c3 = transform.param.constant 3 : i64 -> !transform.param<i64>
  %rank = transform.match.structured.rank %arg : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.param<i64>
  transform.match.param.cmpi eq %rank, %c3 : !transform.param<i64>
  // Check reduction dimension
  transform.match.structured.dim %arg[-1] {reduction} : !transform.any_op
  transform.match.structured.yield %arg : !transform.any_op
}
```

### Match by DAG structure (IREE - cast-compatible types)
```mlir
%ins, %outs = transform.iree.match.cast_compatible_dag_from_root %root {
  ^bb0(%lhs: tensor<?x?xf16>, %rhs: tensor<?x?xf16>, %out: tensor<?x?xf32>):
  %r = linalg.generic {
    indexing_maps = [affine_map<(d0, d1, d2) -> (d0, d2)>,
                     affine_map<(d0, d1, d2) -> (d1, d2)>,
                     affine_map<(d0, d1, d2) -> (d0, d1)>],
    iterator_types = ["parallel", "parallel", "reduction"]}
    ins(%lhs, %rhs : tensor<?x?xf16>, tensor<?x?xf16>)
    outs(%out : tensor<?x?xf32>) {
  ^bb0(%in: f16, %in_0: f16, %acc: f32):
    %0 = arith.extf %in : f16 to f32
    %1 = arith.extf %in_0 : f16 to f32
    %2 = arith.mulf %0, %1 : f32
    %3 = arith.addf %acc, %2 : f32
    linalg.yield %3 : f32
  } -> tensor<?x?xf32>
} : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_value, !transform.any_value)
```

### Match specific tensor shapes
```mlir
%lhs = transform.get_operand %op[0] : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_value
transform.iree.match.cast_compatible_type %lhs = tensor<2048x5120xf16>
  : !transform.any_value
```

### Compose matchers via include
```mlir
transform.named_sequence @match_specific_mmt(
    %op: !transform.any_op {transform.readonly})
  -> (!transform.any_op, !transform.any_param) {
  // First check it's an MMT pattern
  %mmt = transform.include @match_mmt_f16_f16_f32 failures(propagate) (%op)
    : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_op
  // Then check specific shapes
  %lhs = transform.get_operand %op[0] : (!transform.any_op) -> !transform.any_value
  transform.iree.match.cast_compatible_type %lhs = tensor<2048x5120xf16>
    : !transform.any_value
  // Yield with config
  %config = transform.param.constant #iree_codegen.compilation_info<...>
    -> !transform.any_param
  transform.yield %op, %config : !transform.any_op, !transform.any_param
}
```

## Critical rules

1. **Handle invalidation**: After a transform consumes a handle, that handle is dead.
   Re-match if you need to reference the same payload later.

2. **Re-match after structural changes**: After tiling or fusion, the original op
   handle points to the tiled/fused version. If you need the parent func, re-match it.

3. **Order matters**: Tile before fuse. Fuse before vectorize. Vectorize before
   bufferize. Bufferize before GPU mapping. Breaking this order produces invalid IR.

4. **Consumed vs readonly**: Mark operands `{transform.readonly}` in matchers
   (they inspect but don't modify). Mark `{transform.consumed}` in entry points
   that modify IR.

5. **foreach_match semantics**: The matcher/action pairs in `foreach_match` walk
   the IR. Actions should only annotate, not restructure IR, because the walk
   continues after each match.

6. **Type consistency**: Every handle flows through typed SSA. If a transform
   returns `!transform.any_op`, you can pass it where `!transform.any_op` is
   expected. Use `!transform.op<"specific.op">` for compile-time type safety.

7. **yield is mandatory**: Every `transform.named_sequence` must end with
   `transform.yield` (with or without return values matching the signature).

## Pitfalls

The following patterns look reasonable but do not work in mainline IREE.
Avoid them; each one wasted an iteration of the agentic optimizer loop on
2026-04-20.

### `transform.match.operation_attribute` does not exist

There is no `match.operation_attribute` op in the IREE bundled LLVM. A
script that uses it fails to parse - and the library-dir loader swallows
the parse error silently, so the compile proceeds with the default pipeline
and the transform looks like it just produced a no-op. Symptoms: `kernel.o`
byte-identical to baseline, cycle count within noise. Surface the real
error with `--iree-codegen-notify-transform-strategy-application` or by
invoking `iree-compile` directly with the library argument.

To match a specific op within a specific function, match by op name, then
use `transform.get_parent_op {op_name = "func.func"}` and compare the
func's symbol via `transform.include` to a matcher that checks
`transform.match.operation_name`. For one-op-per-dispatch cases (common in
IREE dispatches) just matching the op name is sufficient - each dispatch
compiles independently.

### Unqualified vs `@__transform::` qualified codegen_spec

Both forms end up using only the leaf name (`my_strategy`) at symbol
resolution time (`SymbolRefAttr::getLeafReference()`). Qualifying the
reference adds no lookup power. Use the short form.

### Conv ops without decomposition

As noted in the Conv2D variant above: named conv ops cannot be vectorized
in one shot. Without `transform.structured.decompose` between tile and
vectorize, the strategy fails at the `vectorize_children_and_apply_patterns`
line and the whole dispatch falls back to the default pipeline.

### `transform.iree.bufferize { target_gpu }` on a CPU target

`target_gpu` is a CPU/GPU selector. For CPU strategies, omit the attribute:
`transform.iree.bufferize %func_v : (!transform.any_op) -> (!transform.any_op)`.
Leaving `target_gpu` on a CPU compile produces a GPU-specific buffer
allocation that the CPU pipeline cannot lower.

### Handle threading across tile + decompose

`transform.structured.decompose` produces a fresh handle for the decomposed
op. Don't try to keep using the pre-decompose handle for further tiling -
re-match the resulting linalg.generic by op name if you need to tile it
again.

### Silent library load failures

The `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library[-dir]` flag family does not
emit a diagnostic when the library file fails to parse or verify. The
compile succeeds and uses the default pipeline. When debugging a strategy
that seems to have no effect:

1. Compare `kernel.o` md5 before and after - identical means the transform
   never applied.
2. Re-run with `--iree-codegen-notify-transform-strategy-application`;
   look for `remark: Applied transform configuration strategy @__transform::@__kernel_config`.
   Absence of the remark means the library was silently rejected.
3. Invoke `iree-compile` directly (bypass the harness) with the same
   arguments to see stderr.

## How to use this skill

When given a natural language description of a desired transformation:

1. Identify the **target operations** (matmul, conv, generic, etc.)
2. Identify the **target backend** (GPU with shared memory, CPU, etc.)
3. Determine the **transformation phases** needed
4. Check `references/` for exact op syntax if unsure
5. Write the complete script with proper handle threading
6. Add comments explaining each phase

The output must be a syntactically valid `.mlir` file that can be passed to
`iree-opt` or used as a `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library`.

140	iteration_type (parallel/reduction), tensors (shapes, dtypes), pipeline
141	(CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / etc.),
142	default_tile_sizes, target (cpu, triple, vlen_bytes, features), func_name.
143	
144	Initialize `state.best = null`, `state.iteration = 0`,
145	`state.consecutive_non_improvements = 0`, `state.max_iterations = 15`,
146	`state.max_consecutive_non_improvements = 3`.
147	
148	**Transition:** → `baseline`
149	**Fail:** extract error → write error to state, → `idle`
150	
151	---
152	
153	## State: `baseline`
154	
155	Measure IREE default cycle count and capture the baseline assembly.
156	
157	1. Profile:
158	   ```bash
159	   ./iree_tuner.py <model_path> --profile -s <N> -p <platform_toml> --show-cycle
160	   ```
161	   Parse `module_main_dispatch_<N>\t<cycle_count>` from stdout.
162	
163	2. Disassemble the just-built kernel object:
164	   ```bash
165	   mkdir -p opt/dispatch_<N>/asm
166	   riscv64-elf-objdump -d <working_dir>/temp/kernel.o > opt/dispatch_<N>/asm/baseline.s
167	   ```
168	
169	3. Update state:
170	   ```
171	   baseline = {cycles, compile_time_s, run_time_s, asm_file: "opt/dispatch_<N>/asm/baseline.s"}
172	   best = {cycles: baseline.cycles, iteration: 0, transform_file: null,
173	           asm_file: baseline.asm_file, description: "IREE default (<pipeline>)"}
174	   ```
175	
176	**Transition:** → `analyze` (round 1)
177	**Fail:** baseline compile/run error → record in state, → `idle`
178	
179	---
180	
181	## State: `analyze`
182	
183	Form the next hypothesis. Delegated to the **strategy-proposer subagent**.
184	
185	**Gatekeeper.** If the current best assembly already achieves full VLmax with
186	LMUL≥m4, no register spills in the hot loop, and high vector:scalar ratio,
187	and the history shows multiple failed attempts to improve - output
188	`"no clear opportunity"` and transition out.
189	
190	### Subagent inputs
191	
192	Pass these to the proposer explicitly:
193	
194	1. `state.ir_analysis` (op type, shapes, dtypes, iteration space, default_tile_sizes, target)
195	2. `state.best.asm_file` contents (the best known assembly so far)
196	3. `state.best.transform_file` contents if non-null (the best transform, as a starting point for evolution)
197	4. `state.history` (every prior iteration: hypothesis, transform_file, cycles, outcome, insight)
198	5. `state.baseline.cycles` + `state.best.cycles` (the bar to beat)
199	6. Cross-dispatch winners: `ls opt/dispatch_*/state.json` filtered for same `op_type` and similar shapes - their winning strategies may transfer
200	
201	### What the proposer must extract from the assembly
202	
203	Run these greps on `state.best.asm_file` to ground the proposal:
204	
205	| Metric | Command | Interpretation |
206	|--------|---------|----------------|
207	| LMUL / SEW | `grep -E 'vsetvli\|vsetivli' <asm>` | `e32,m4` → 32-bit, LMUL=4. VLmax = VLEN*LMUL/SEW elements. |
208	| Vector op density | `grep -cE '^\s+[0-9a-f]+:\s+.*\bv[a-z]+' <asm>` vs total lines | Low ratio → scalar loop overhead dominates |
209	| Load:compute ratio | `grep -cE '\bvle[0-9]+\.v\b' <asm>` vs `grep -cE '\bvfmacc\|vfmul\|vmacc\|vmul\|vfadd' <asm>` | High load ratio → memory-bound, tile K larger for reuse |
210	| Register spills | `grep -E 'vse[0-9]+\.v.*(sp)\|(s0)' <asm>` | Non-empty in hot loop → reduce LMUL or shrink tiles |
211	| Loop nesting | count of `bne\|bnez\|blt` backedges in function body | Compare to ir_analysis.iteration_space rank - missing a level = not tiled there |
212	
213	### Example observation → candidate direction
214	
215	These are illustrative only. The proposer derives the actual hypothesis from the
216	specific asm + ir_analysis of the current dispatch, not by pattern-matching this
217	table. Every hypothesis must cite the asm lines that justify it.
218	
219	- Low LMUL with no spills → push toward wider vector grouping
220	- Inner tile smaller than VLmax → push toward fuller vector lane utilization
221	- Innermost iteration is a reduction with poor vector op density → push toward making a parallel dim innermost
222	- High load:compute ratio → push toward more operand reuse per load
223	- Spills under wide LMUL → push toward less register pressure
224	- Scalar ops dominate hot loop → push toward amortizing scalar overhead across more vector work
225	- Asm already dense with vector ops and no spills, plus prior attempts regressed → output "no clear opportunity"
226	
227	### Subagent output
228	
229	1-3 ranked hypotheses. **The proposer describes the diagnosis, not the
230	prescription.** It must NOT name specific transform dialect ops
231	(`tile_using_for`, `vectorize`, etc.) - that is the writer's job. Each
232	hypothesis is a JSON object:
233	
234	```json
235	{
236	  "metric_issue": "<what the asm shows is wrong, citing specific lines and the
237	                   numbers that prove it. e.g. 'asm lines 142-189: 24 vle32.v
238	                   vs 8 vfmacc in the hot loop, load:compute = 3:1, memory-bound'>",
239	  "improvement_direction": "<the direction to push, in metric terms, not
240	                            transform-op terms. e.g. 'increase operand reuse
241	                            along the reduction dimension so each loaded vector
242	                            feeds more FMA ops'>",
243	  "expected_metric_change": "<the asm-observable change that would confirm
244	                             the hypothesis worked. e.g. 'load:compute drops
245	                             to ~1:1; vle count roughly halves; instruction
246	                             count may rise from epilogue but net cycles fall'>",
247	  "risk": "<compilation failure likelihood + regression likelihood, with
248	           reasoning grounded in ir_analysis (e.g. 'reduction-dim tiling on
249	           CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert is fragile - high compile-failure
250	           risk') >"
251	}
252	```
253	
254	Pick top. Record `state.current_iter = {hypothesis, compile_retries: 0}` where
255	`hypothesis` is the JSON object above (not a free-form string).
256	
257	**Transition:** → `implement` (hypothesis chosen) | → `evaluate` via `restore_best` (no opportunity)
258	
259	---
260	
261	## State: `implement`
262	
263	Generate the transform script. Delegated to the **mlir-transform-writer skill**.
264	
265	### Subagent inputs
266	
267	The optimizer hands the writer a **problem statement**, not a recipe. The writer
268	owns all decisions about which transform dialect ops to use and in what order.
269	
270	1. `state.current_iter.hypothesis.metric_issue` - what the current asm shows is
271	   wrong, with citations.
272	2. `state.current_iter.hypothesis.improvement_direction` - the direction to push,
273	   stated in metric/IR terms (not transform-op terms).
274	3. `state.current_iter.hypothesis.expected_metric_change` - the asm-observable
275	   change that would confirm success. The writer can use this as a sanity check.
276	4. `state.ir_analysis` (full structure: op_type, iteration_space,
277	   iteration_type, tensors, pipeline, default_tile_sizes, target, func_name).
278	5. `state.best.transform_file` contents if non-null (evolution starting point).
279	   `null` means start from the IREE default pipeline.
280	6. `state.best.asm_file` contents - the writer can re-read the asm to confirm
281	   the diagnosis before committing to a transform.
282	7. Format contract: `TransformDialectCodegen` named_sequence with `codegen_spec`.
283	
284	The writer is free to:
285	- Pick any combination of transform ops (tiling, fusion, vectorization,
286	  bufferization, GPU mapping, etc.) that addresses the `improvement_direction`.
287	- Reject the hypothesis if the asm/IR contradicts it (return
288	  `implement_error` with a 1-2 sentence justification - the optimizer will
289	  surface this back to `analyze`).
290	- Choose tile sizes / LMUL / loop order based on its own knowledge of the
291	  target (`state.ir_analysis.target.vlen_bits`, `features`, etc.).
292	
293	### Output
294	
295	Write transform to `opt/dispatch_<N>/transforms/iter_M.mlir` where
296	`M = state.iteration + 1`.
297	
298	**Transition:** → `compile_measure`
299	**Fail:** mlir-transform-writer refuses or errors → record in history as
300	"implement_error" with the writer's justification, → `analyze` for a different
301	hypothesis.
302	
303	---
304	
305	## State: `compile_measure`
306	
307	Compile with the new transform, benchmark, disassemble.
308	
309	1. Install the transform:
310	   ```bash
311	   mkdir -p <working_dir>/transform_library
312	   cp opt/dispatch_<N>/transforms/iter_M.mlir \
313	      <working_dir>/transform_library/transform_library_dispatch_<N>.mlir
314	   ```
315	
316	2. Snapshot the current kernel.o md5 **before** compiling. This is the
317	   guardrail for silent library-load failures:
318	   ```bash
319	   md5_before=$(md5sum <working_dir>/temp/kernel.o 2>/dev/null | awk '{print $1}')
320	   ```
321	
322	3. Compile + run:
323	   ```bash
324	   ./iree_tuner.py <model_path> --profile -s <N> -p <platform_toml> \
325	     --with-transform --show-cycle
326	   ```
327	
328	4. Parse:
329	   - Success exit + stdout has `module_main_dispatch_<N>\t<cycles>` → candidate cycles recorded
330	   - Compile error exit → stderr has compile failure; capture first 500 chars
331	   - Run error → stderr has run failure; treat as regression with `cycles=null`
332	
333	5. **Silent-no-op check** (IREE's `--iree-codegen-transform-dialect-library[-dir]`
334	   does not emit a diagnostic when a library fails to parse - the compile
335	   succeeds with the default pipeline). After a "success":
336	   ```bash
337	   md5_after=$(md5sum <working_dir>/temp/kernel.o | awk '{print $1}')
338	   ```
339	   If `md5_after == md5_before` AND `state.best.asm_file` exists AND
340	   `md5sum <working_dir>/temp/kernel.o` equals
341	   `md5sum <<(objdump-hash-of-baseline)>>` (i.e. byte-identical to the
342	   last known kernel), then the transform was silently ignored. Treat this
343	   as a compile error with the synthetic message:
344	   `"silent no-op: kernel.o unchanged from previous build; transform library likely rejected during parse/verify"`.
345	   Re-run the underlying iree-compile command manually with
346	   `--iree-codegen-notify-transform-strategy-application` to surface the
347	   real error and capture it for `error_fix`.
348	
349	6. On a real success, disassemble with a clobbering redirect (plain `>` is
350	   blocked by zsh `noclobber`):
351	   ```bash
352	   riscv64-elf-objdump -d <working_dir>/temp/kernel.o \
353	     >| opt/dispatch_<N>/asm/iter_M.s
354	   ```
355	
356	**Transition (success):** → `learn`
357	**Transition (compile error or silent no-op):** → `error_fix` (bump `state.current_iter.compile_retries`)
358	**Transition (run error):** → `learn` (treat as regression, outcome=error)
359	
360	---
361	
362	## State: `error_fix`
363	
364	Fix the transform in place. Delegated to the **transform-error-fixer subagent**.
365	
366	### Subagent inputs
367	
368	1. `opt/dispatch_<N>/transforms/iter_M.mlir` (broken transform)
369	2. The compile error text (truncated to 500 chars in state, full text in last command output)
370	3. `state.ir_analysis` (for context on op types, handle types)
371	
372	### Common error classes
373	
374	- **Handle invalidation**: `consumed handle reused` - a transform op consumed a handle, subsequent uses need a re-match
375	- **Type mismatch**: `wrong handle type` - `any_op` vs `op<"linalg.matmul">`, fix the type annotation
376	- **Op not found**: `op not found in payload` - the match pattern is too narrow, relax it
377	- **Bufferization failure**: missing `one_shot_bufferize` or wrong order
378	
379	### Output
380	
381	Edit `iter_M.mlir` in place (same file path, no new iter_M+1). Return:
382	
383	- The edit description (1-2 sentences explaining what was wrong and why the
384	  fix works). This is consumed by `skill_feedback` so it can decide whether
385	  the lesson is worth teaching to the transform writer.
386	- The error class (one of: `HandleInvalidation`, `TypeMismatch`,
387	  `OpNotFound`, `BufferizationFailure`, `SilentNoOp`, `MissingDecompose`,
388	  `UnknownOp`, or `Other`). Pattern-matched against the error text.
389	
390	**Transition:** → `skill_feedback` (unconditional - always pass through, so
391	the lesson is captured before retry or abandon).
392	
393	---
394	
395	## State: `skill_feedback`
396	
397	Compound-effect step: every compile failure in `error_fix` is a concrete
398	datum about how the transform writer misfires. Capture the lesson once,
399	not every iteration. Long-term this shrinks `error_fix` to a no-op because
400	the same error class never recurs.
401	
402	### Inputs
403	
404	1. `state.current_iter.hypothesis`
405	2. `state.current_iter.error` (first 500 chars of stderr, set by
406	   `compile_measure`)
407	3. `state.current_iter.error_class` (set by `error_fix`)
408	4. `state.current_iter.fix_description` (set by `error_fix`)
409	5. The pre/post diff of `opt/dispatch_<N>/transforms/iter_M.mlir` (obtain
410	   via `git diff --no-index <pre> <post>` or by diffing against the
411	   previous iter_M if available)
412	6. `~/.claude/skills/mlir-transform-writer/SKILL.md` - the target of any
413	   update; specifically its `## Pitfalls` section
414	
415	### Algorithm
416	
417	1. **Novelty check.** Grep the mlir-transform-writer SKILL.md for the
418	   error_class keyword AND the key phrase from the error text. If both
419	   appear in an existing Pitfalls subsection, skip - the lesson is
420	   already there.
421	2. **Scope check.** The lesson only applies to mlir-transform-writer if
422	   it is *authoring-related* (an op that doesn't exist, a pattern order
423	   requirement, a missing decompose step, a required closing annotation,
424	   etc.). Infrastructure failures (flag incompatibility, toolchain path
425	   issues) belong in the iree-optimizer skill's own prerequisites or in
426	   harness code - not in mlir-transform-writer.
427	3. **Emit the update.** If novel and in-scope, append a new subsection
428	   under `## Pitfalls` in `~/.claude/skills/mlir-transform-writer/SKILL.md`:
429	
430	   ```
431	   ### <short title, e.g. "Missing decompose before vectorize">
432	
433	   <1-2 sentences describing the failure: what error the writer sees, and
434	   under which IR preconditions it happens.>
435	
436	   Error signature: `<the distinctive substring from the compile error,
437	   trimmed to a grep-friendly length>`
438	
439	   Fix: <1-2 sentences on the corrective edit, citing the transform op
440	   that resolves it.>
441	   ```
442	
443	   Use the `Edit` tool with `old_string = "## How to use this skill"` and
444	   `new_string = "<new subsection>\n\n## How to use this skill"` to insert
445	   before the final section. Never overwrite the whole file.
446	4. **Record the event.** Append to `state.skill_feedback_log`:
447	   ```json
448	   {
449	     "iteration": M,
450	     "error_class": "...",
451	     "error_signature": "...",
452	     "action": "appended_pitfall" | "skipped_known" | "skipped_out_of_scope",
453	     "timestamp": "2026-04-20T12:34:56"
454	   }
455	   ```
456	
457	### Constraints
458	
459	- **Do not call this state's logic recursively.** If the novelty check is
460	  ambiguous (close match but not identical), prefer `skipped_known` over
461	  appending a near-duplicate.
462	- **Authoring lessons only.** Harness bugs (wrong cl flag, missing
463	  toolchain, Python errors in iree_tuner.py) must not land in
464	  mlir-transform-writer - they don't belong to that skill's scope.
465	- **One writer SKILL edit per optimizer run per dispatch at most.** If
466	  multiple iterations hit the same error class, the first one teaches the
467	  writer; subsequent iterations see the novelty check return "known" and
468	  skip. This keeps the skill from accumulating near-duplicate entries.
469	
470	### Transitions
471	
472	- **From `error_fix` with retry<3:** → `compile_measure` (re-run with the
473	  patched transform). The fix is already on disk.
474	- **From `error_fix` with retries exhausted:** record iter_M as
475	  `outcome=error` in history, run `restore_best`, → `analyze` with a
476	  different approach. The feedback is already recorded regardless.
477	
478	---
479	
480	## State: `learn`
481	
482	Record the round, update best, synthesize insight, decide next move.
483	
484	1. Compute outcome:
485	   - `cycles < best.cycles` → `outcome="improved"`, update `state.best`, reset `consecutive_non_improvements = 0`
486	   - `cycles == best.cycles` (within 1% noise) → `outcome="neutral"`, bump `consecutive_non_improvements`
487	   - `cycles > best.cycles` → `outcome="regressed"`, bump `consecutive_non_improvements`
488	   - compile/run error through → `outcome="error"`, bump `consecutive_non_improvements`
489	
490	2. Synthesize the round's **insight** (1-2 sentences): what the assembly revealed
491	   about why this outcome happened. Examples:
492	   - "Wider tiling increased VLmax utilization but caused register spills at m8; needs m4."
493	   - "Loop interchange moved N innermost but the pipeline now has VLSU stalls from non-contiguous stores."
494	   - "K-tile enlarged reduced vle count by 50% but instruction count rose due to extra epilogue; net regression."
495	
496	3. Append to `state.history`:
497	   ```json
498	   {
499	     "iteration": M,
500	     "hypothesis": "...",
501	     "transform_file": "opt/dispatch_N/transforms/iter_M.mlir",
502	     "asm_file": "opt/dispatch_N/asm/iter_M.s",
503	     "cycles": 247000000,
504	     "outcome": "improved|regressed|neutral|error",
505	     "insight": "...",
506	     "error": null
507	   }
508	   ```
509	
510	4. Increment `state.iteration`. Clear `state.current_iter`.
511	
512	5. Check convergence:
513	   - `iteration >= max_iterations` → `restore_best` → `evaluate`
514	   - `consecutive_non_improvements >= max_consecutive_non_improvements` → `restore_best` → `evaluate`
515	   - Otherwise → `analyze` (round M+1; the updated asm + insight seed the next hypothesis)
516	
517	**Transition:** → `analyze` or `evaluate`
518	
519	---
520	
521	## State: `evaluate`
522	
523	Confirm the best result.
524	
525	1. `restore_best` (copy `state.best.transform_file` into working_dir, or clear it for baseline)
526	2. Re-run compile + benchmark exactly as `compile_measure` (but don't bump iteration)
527	3. Verify cycles match `state.best.cycles` within 1%. Record discrepancy if not.
528	4. Print summary:
529	
530	```
531	Dispatch <N>: <op_type> <shapes>
532	Baseline: <baseline.cycles> cycles
533	Best:     <best.cycles> cycles   (iter <best.iteration>, speedup <ratio>x)
534	Winner:   <best.description>
535	Transform: <best.transform_file>
536	
537	Round history:
538	round | outcome  | cycles     | insight
539	1     | regressed| 360067008  | LMUL=m2 but scalar overhead dominant
540	2     | neutral  | 260551732  | Mirrored default; no room for tile tweaks
541	3     | improved | 247219040  | Raised LMUL to m4, tiled N=16 for VLmax=16
542	...
543	```
544	
545	**Transition:** → `idle`
546

629	
630	## Subagent Dispatch
631	
632	The main agent drives the FSM and reports progress between every state.
633	Reasoning steps use subagents; mechanical steps run inline.
634	
635	| State | Execution | Why |
636	|-------|-----------|-----|
637	| idle / gather / baseline | main | shell commands only |
638	| **analyze** | subagent (strategy-proposer) | reads asm + ir_analysis + history, forms hypothesis |
639	| **implement** | subagent (mlir-transform-writer skill) | generates transform .mlir with domain knowledge |
640	| compile_measure | main | shell commands, parse stdout/stderr, kernel.o md5 guardrail |
641	| **error_fix** | subagent (transform-error-fixer) | diagnoses compile error, edits transform, returns error class + fix description |
642	| **skill_feedback** | main | novelty-check the error class, append a Pitfall to mlir-transform-writer SKILL.md if new |
643	| learn | main | record outcome, synthesize insight, decide |
644	| evaluate | main | restore_best, re-run, print summary |
645	
646	### Subagent constraints
647	
648	**strategy-proposer** (analyze):
649	- Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (only `grep`/`head`/`wc` on `.s` files)
650	- MUST NOT: edit files, run compile/benchmark, read mlir-transform docs, name
651	  specific transform dialect ops in the hypothesis
652	- MUST: read `state.best.asm_file`, extract LMUL/VLmax/spill metrics, cite
653	  specific asm lines when justifying a hypothesis
654	- Output: 1-3 ranked hypotheses, each as the `{metric_issue,
655	  improvement_direction, expected_metric_change, risk}` JSON object defined
656	  above, OR `"no clear opportunity"` with justification. The hypothesis
657	  describes the *problem* and the *direction* in metric terms - it never
658	  prescribes which transform ops to use (that is the writer's choice).
659	
660	**mlir-transform-writer skill** (implement):
661	- Invoked via Skill tool or by reading `~/.claude/skills/mlir-transform-writer/SKILL.md`
662	- Receives the hypothesis as a problem statement (metric_issue +
663	  improvement_direction + expected_metric_change), not as a recipe. Owns all
664	  decisions about which transform ops to compose.
665	- Writes `opt/dispatch_<N>/transforms/iter_M.mlir` only
666	- Follows the TransformDialectCodegen format (named_sequence + codegen_spec)
667	- May reject the hypothesis if asm/IR contradicts it, returning a 1-2
668	  sentence justification (recorded as `implement_error` in history)
669	
670	**transform-error-fixer** (error_fix):
671	- Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
672	- MUST NOT: run any Bash commands, generate new iter_M+1 files
673	- MUST: edit `iter_M.mlir` in place, based on the compile error class
674	- Output: `{fix_description: str, error_class: str}` - consumed by `skill_feedback`
675	  to decide whether to teach `mlir-transform-writer`
676	
677	---
678	
679	## Rules
680	
681	1. **Fully autonomous.** Do not wait for user confirmation between states.
682	2. **Assembly is ground truth.** Every round reads the current best's `.s` file. Never propose strategies without citing specific asm evidence.
683	3. **FPGA is the scoreboard.** Only `config/AX45_1024.toml` (or AX45_512) provides cycle counts. QEMU is correctness-only.
684	4. **Stable storage of transforms.** Every `opt/dispatch_<N>/transforms/iter_M.mlir` is kept forever. Never overwrite. `<working_dir>/transform_library/` is scratch.
685	5. **Max 15 iterations per dispatch.** Enforced in `learn`.
686	6. **Stop after 3 consecutive non-improvements.** Enforced in `learn`.
687	7. **Max 3 compile retries per iteration.** Enforced in `error_fix`. Then restore_best and let analyze try a different approach.
688	8. **analyze is the gatekeeper.** If asm shows no clear opportunity, exit via evaluate - do not force pointless changes.
689	9. **Always restore_best before evaluate/exit** so the user's next run picks up the winning transform.
690	10. **Record wins AND losses.** The `history` drives the next analyze; losses prevent repeating dead ends.
691	11. **Cross-dispatch learning.** analyze reads `opt/dispatch_*/state.json` for same-op-type winners. A transform that worked on dispatch X often transfers to dispatch Y with similar shapes.
692	12. **Never invoke iree-compile directly.** Always go through `iree_tuner.py`.
693	13. **Run everything from AutoIREE root.** Paths in state are relative to that.
694	14. **Conv2D is hard.** The default `CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert` pipeline does specialized decomposition TransformDialectCodegen cannot replicate. If baseline wins on conv2d after 3 rounds, accept and move on.
695	15. **Do not edit iter_M.mlir after it is measured.** Only the error_fix state edits an iter_M, and only before it has successfully compiled.
696	16. **Compound effect: teach the writer, don't just fix.** Every compile error routes through `skill_feedback`. If the error class is authoring-related and not already in `mlir-transform-writer`'s Pitfalls section, append a short subsection so the next run avoids the same mistake. Harness/infrastructure failures (flags, toolchain, harness Python bugs) are out-of-scope for `mlir-transform-writer` and must not be written there - they go in this skill's Prerequisites or in harness code.
697	17. **Silent no-op is a compile error.** If `kernel.o` is byte-identical to the pre-compile snapshot after a "successful" build, treat it as a compile error and route to `error_fix` with the synthetic message above. This catches silently-rejected transform libraries that IREE's library-dir loader does not diagnose.
698	
699	---
700	
701	## Strategy Guide
702	
703	Full knowledge: `~/.claude/skills/iree-optimizer/references/strategy_guide.md`.
704	
705	### CPU (RISC-V with RVV) - quick reference
706	
707	- VLEN is in **bits** in the platform TOML. `native_vector_size` in MLIR is in **bytes** (VLEN/8).
708	- VLmax per vector op = `VLEN * LMUL / SEW` (in elements). For VLEN=1024, e32 (f32), LMUL=m4: VLmax = 1024*4/32 = 128 elements.
709	- LMUL values (m1/m2/m4/m8): higher widens each vector op at the cost of register budget; the right value depends on live-range count visible in the current asm.
710	- Tiling levels have distinct purposes:
711	  - Distribution / outer tiles: cache blocking
712	  - Inner / vector tiles: vector register utilization
713	  - Reduction tiles: operand reuse vs register pressure
714	- Mapping iteration dims to tile levels is op- and shape-specific. Derive it from asm evidence (LMUL, VLmax, spills, load:compute ratio) + ir_analysis (iteration_space, iteration_type, memory layout). Do not apply a fixed recipe.
715	- `CPUDoubleTilingExpert` expects 6 tiling levels; `CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert` fewer. Match the pipeline's level count.
716	
717	### State schema reference
718	
719	`~/.claude/skills/iree-optimizer/references/state_schema.md` (legacy schema_version=1; this SKILL uses schema_version=2 above).
720
